card.title: The Eisenhower Urgency-Importance Matrix
card.teaser: A five-star general's private trick for beating the tyranny of the urgent — now used by CEOs, parents, and presidents alike.
card.coverImagePrompt: A general's wartime desk with a leather blotter, a mug of coffee, and a handwritten 2x2 grid on yellowed paper, warm amber light spilling through a tent flap, cinematic

lessons.title: A general's quiet confession
lessons.body: In a 1954 speech, President Dwight Eisenhower quoted an unnamed university president: 'I have two kinds of problems, the urgent and the important. The urgent are not important, and the important are never urgent.' It was a throwaway line — but it captured the entire philosophy of how Eisenhower ran a war, then a country.

visual.imagePrompt: A black-and-white photograph of Eisenhower at a podium, mid-speech, archival photojournalism style, slight film grain

lessons.title: Two axes, four boxes
lessons.body: Stephen Covey popularized the general's insight as a simple 2x2 grid. One axis: urgent vs. not urgent. The other: important vs. not important. Every task drops into one quadrant. Quadrant 1: urgent and important — do it now. Quadrant 2: important but not urgent — schedule it. Quadrant 3: urgent but not important — delegate. Quadrant 4: neither — drop it.

keyTerms.definition: Demands immediate attention, often imposed by others
Contributes to your long-term mission, values, or goals

visual.imagePrompt: A clean minimalist 2x2 matrix with hand-drawn labels: Do, Schedule, Delegate, Delete — warm paper texture

lessons.title: The quadrant that runs your life
lessons.body: Covey observed that people default to Quadrant 3 — urgent-but-not-important tasks like reactive emails and interruptions. It feels productive because it's busy. Meanwhile Quadrant 2 — strategic planning, learning, relationships — is where real leadership lives, and it gets squeezed out. The best operators protect Q2 time like it's sacred, because it is.
lessons.funFact: Eisenhower was famous for leaving blank hours on his official calendar labeled simply 'thinking time.' His staff were forbidden to book into them.

visual.imagePrompt: A CEO's calendar with most slots filled except two large blocked-off rectangles labeled 'thinking time,' flat design

lessons.title: Ask two questions, not a hundred
lessons.body: Next time your inbox feels like chaos, don't write a to-do list — run the matrix. 'Is this urgent? Is this important?' Two yes-or-no answers narrow every item to one of four actions. The magic isn't in the grid; it's in the discipline of pausing long enough to ask. Leaders who skip the question live in Q3 forever.

quiz.question: Which quadrant should you schedule rather than react to?
quiz.explanation: Quadrant 2 — important but not urgent — is where strategic leadership lives. It requires deliberate scheduling because nothing forces it on you.

quiz.question: Eisenhower credited the urgent/important distinction to:
quiz.explanation: In his 1954 speech, Eisenhower attributed the line to an unnamed college president, though the idea became permanently associated with him.

quiz.question: The trap most people fall into is living in:
quiz.explanation: Q3 tasks feel productive because they're urgent, but they're not what actually moves the needle. They squeeze out Q2, where real progress happens.

sources.title: Address at the Second Assembly of the World Council of Churches
The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People
